summary: Gruber articulates why comparing Safari to IE 6 is a flawed analogy, arguing that IE 6's dominance allowed Microsoft to unilaterally define the web, whereas today's browser landscape is a four-way power split. He frames browser vendors as analogous to UN Security Council permanent members, each holding de facto veto power over web standards through their market share. Apple's position stands out not because it has more power than others, but because its priorities have diverged from web developers who want mobile web apps to rival native apps. Gruber argues this is simply realpolitik: no community pressure can force any browser maker to act against its self-interest, and standards only become real when all major engines implement them.
key_points: IE 6 didn't just influence the web — it effectively was the web, making the Safari-as-new-IE comparison fundamentally flawed since no single engine dominates today
All four major browser engines (WebKit, Blink, Trident/Edge, Gecko) hold de facto veto power over web standards, not just Apple
Apple's divergence from certain web developers stems from differing interests in the web's direction, not disinterest in the web itself
Real web standards are defined not by W3C specifications but by what is consistently implemented across all major rendering engines
Market pressure is the only mechanism that can force a browser maker to adopt a standard it resists — community complaints alone accomplish nothing
money_quotes: IE 6 was so popular that it allowed Microsoft to define the web. It was as though IE 6 was the web.
There are a lot of nerds — and I use that term affectionately, not pejoratively — for whom politics of any sort are just anathema.
It isn't whether it is right or wrong, fair or unfair, that they have this power. It's simply to point out that they have this veto power.
WebKit's contrary priorities are not the result of disinterest in the web; they are the result of differing interest in the web.
A nuclear weapon arsenal gives each country geopolitical influence that cannot be denied or ignored. Might makes right.
The real web is not that which is defined by the W3C as a standard, but that which is implemented in a consistent manner across WebKit, Blink, Trident, and Gecko.
The secret to the web's wonderful success is that it's a (nearly) universal meta-platform; that which is not implemented on a major platform, like, say, iOS, is by definition not universal.
I'm not saying that's good or bad. It just is how it is.
key_insight: Every major browser engine holds de facto veto power over web standards through market share, and Apple's exercise of that power reflects divergent priorities rather than unique dominance — a political reality no amount of community frustration can override.
